title: Carlsen vs Caruana — 2018 WCC, Game 11
description: Carlsen surprises with 1.e4 in a Berlin-style line, but Caruana forces the queens off and liquidates to a comfortable, textbook opposite-colored-bishop draw. Annotated by GM Sam Shankland.

conceptChecks.prompt: Caruana plays 12...Qa5 ("!"). What does this move accomplish?

options.text: It forces the queens off the board, steering toward a safe, simplified position.
options.explanation: Correct. The note reads: "This forces the queens off. Black has to be a little careful I guess." Indeed 13.c4 Qxd2 14.Bxd2 follows.

options.text: It traps White's queen in the center.
options.explanation: No — the annotator makes no such claim; the queen is simply traded, not trapped.

options.text: It launches a kingside mating attack.
options.explanation: The opposite — Black is heading for mass simplification, and the annotator even warns Black 'has to be a little careful.'

conceptChecks.hint: Look at what happens on the very next move.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator gives 15.Nh4 a "?!". What is his assessment of this try?

options.text: It's too forcing and essentially just leads to a draw; the patient 15.h3 was a better winning attempt.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "This is very forcing, extremely direct, and basically just a draw." Of 15.h3 he writes, "A patient move like this one was certainly a better try."

options.text: It wins material by force against the e6-bishop.
options.explanation: No such claim — the annotator says the line is drawish, not materially winning.

options.text: It is the only way for White to keep his advantage.
options.explanation: The reverse: the annotator notes the patient 15.h3 even gave White 'a slight edge,' while Nh4 hands the advantage away.

conceptChecks.hint: Was this the patient choice or the forcing one?

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator mark 18...Ne5 with a "!"?

options.text: It halves White's bishop pair and liquidates everything into a simple, equal position.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "The bishop pair is halved, and everything liquidates in short order."

options.text: It traps and wins the d3-bishop.
options.explanation: No — the annotator describes simplification and liquidation, not winning a piece.

options.text: It sets up a direct attack on White's king.
options.explanation: No; Black is simplifying toward a draw, not attacking.

conceptChecks.hint: Think about which advantage of White's gets neutralized.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator calls 20...Rd7 ("!") "the last accurate move" that "ends the game." Why?

options.text: White's only target — the d6-pawn — now gets exchanged off, snuffing out any winning chances.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "The last accurate move ends the game. The d6 pawn could have been a target, and now it will be exchanged."

options.text: It wins the exchange by trapping a white rook.
options.explanation: No — the point is defusing the d6 weakness, not winning material.

options.text: It prepares a decisive kingside attack.
options.explanation: The annotator frames it as the move that secures the draw, not an attacking idea.

conceptChecks.hint: What was White's one potential target?

conceptChecks.prompt: Deep in the drawn endgame Caruana plays 54...Bb1 ("!"). What is the point?

options.text: It keeps the fortress intact — ...Bxf5 is now available, so White's Kxf7 idea no longer works.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Now Kxf7 is no longer an issue since it can be met with Bxf5," sidestepping the fxg6 trick that would otherwise win.

options.text: It wins White's passed h-pawn by force.
options.explanation: No — the note is purely about holding the draw, not winning a pawn.

options.text: It begins a winning plan for Black.
options.explanation: The position is a dead draw; Black is only maintaining the fortress.

conceptChecks.hint: What new defensive resource does putting the bishop on the b1-h7 diagonal grant?
